title: Mobile malware survey — credential theft, premium SMS, 2FA bypass, IMEI defenses
html:
TL;DR

The continuation page of Felt et al. — the page where the money math of mobile malware gets concrete. Three big things land here:

  1. Black-market price sheet for stolen credentials, from Symantec 2008. Bank credentials $10–$1,000; credit cards $0.10–$25; email account passwords $4–$30. Legitimate ad libraries earn $1.90–$9.50 per user per month — the realistic upper bound on what a single user's data is worth. (credential black market pricing)

  2. The first documented two factor authentication bypass in the wild — Spitmo and ZeusMitmo work in tandem with desktop banking trojans. Desktop side phishes the user, tricks them into installing the mobile component, mobile component intercepts the SMS one-time-code. Two-factor-via-SMS was already structurally broken by 2011.

  3. premium rate sms fraud dominates the dataset — 24 of 46 samples. The proposed defense: per-message user confirmation, which iOS already required and which is partially why iOS malware was nearly nonexistent.

Why it's in the graph

The 2008 credential prices are an under-cited fact. They give Yahir a way to talk about the actual economic logic of any data-leak headline — what does this database get sold for? — which is more useful than vague "protect your data" advice. Stash the table.

The 2FA-via-SMS critique is the one to remember in any future rankett-adjacent product that touches auth. SMS 2FA is defense in depth, not defense in fact. Even FIDO/passkey marketing in 2026 is downstream of arguments first made in this paper.

What's actually on the page

  • Defense for IMEI theft: an alternate, globally-unique device ID for legitimate apps, so the real IMEI never gets exposed.
  • Inter-app data theft as a future concern — Symbian and Android allowed cross-app reads; iOS's segregation model was already the right answer.
  • Credential theft incentives with the price sheet.
  • Spitmo + ZeusMitmo as the proof-of-concept attack on banking 2FA.
  • Phishing on phones — convincing because UI surfaces are smaller and trust signals are harder to read.
  • Premium rate SMS — 24/46 samples; iOS-style per-message confirmation as the universal fix.

Connections worth keeping

  • The price sheet is great companion data for credential black market pricing — and connects loosely to anything Yahir thinks about regarding fraud, KYC, or cold-email reputation (see 2025 09 24 unspam deliverability test).
  • The "iOS already does this" pattern across both pages of the paper is interesting: Apple won the early mobile security era by requiring permissions at the right moments. Worth keeping that thread near disruption — sometimes the disruptor wins the platform war by being annoying in the right places.
  • The Spitmo/ZeusMitmo workflow (desktop phish → mobile install) is the structural ancestor of every modern multi-stage phishing campaign. Same shape, different rails.
